summary: The paper studies how to evaluate functional similarity between independently trained neural networks via model stitching. It observes that standard stitching only enforces "forward compatibility" — that one model's representations can be aligned through a stitching layer into another model and still solve the task — and that this criterion can report high similarity even when the two models rely on entirely different input cues (e.g., shortcut features), a failure mode highlighted in recent work. The authors argue that this blindness stems from stitching ignoring the invariance structure of the stitched models. They introduce a "forward-backward compatibility" requirement and an invariance-aware stitching procedure that additionally trains the alignment on identically-represented inputs (IRIs / metamers) — inputs the front model maps to (nearly) the same intermediate representation — so that the stitched model must handle the front model's invariances consistently with the end model. They instantiate this with a new objective (FuLA) and compare it against several standard stitching formulations (hard- and soft-label matching, direct matching) on vision classifiers. Empirically, they show that standard stitching is inflated by unseen shortcut cues, that invariance-aware stitching mitigates this and lowers spurious similarity, and that under the stricter requirement adversarially robust and non-robust models appear less compatible than forward-only analyses had suggested.
strengths_and_weaknesses:
The paper targets a genuinely important and under-served problem: stitching-based functional-similarity metrics are too permissive, and the observation that a stitched model can achieve high alignment while the two models rely on entirely different information cues is both real and consequential. The conceptual move of separating plain forward compatibility from an invariance-sensitive requirement is a useful contribution, and the shortcut-feature demonstration (where models using different cues nonetheless look highly similar under hard-label-matching stitching) makes the motivation concrete and persuasive. On soundness, the empirical work is solid: the claims are generally well supported, the experiments reasonably isolate the factors of interest, and the study compares several stitching objectives rather than a single formulation. The robust-versus-non-robust analysis across architectures is a well-chosen test case, and revisiting prior conclusions about these models under the stricter criterion is one of the paper's more interesting results. On significance, because ground-truth similarity is essentially unavailable, grounding metrics in intuition and exhibiting concrete failure modes is exactly the kind of methodological hygiene the representation-similarity and model-merging communities need, and the paper makes a meaningful step in that direction.

The central weakness is presentation. The exposition is dense and heavily acronym-laden (TLM, SLM, HLM, DM, FuLA, IRIs), the narrative does not flow easily, and several core notions — forward compatibility, backward compatibility, and what precisely qualifies as "invariance-aware" stitching — are hard to parse on a first read. The "forward-backward compatibility" terminology is itself a source of confusion, since "backward" naturally suggests gradient flow rather than the intended invariance-sharing notion; a name such as "invariance-aware forward compatibility" would be clearer. Relatedly, the paper does not state its main contribution sharply enough: it is not obvious whether the core claim is the compatibility requirement, the IRI-based training setup, the FuLA objective, or the empirical comparison, and this should be made explicit early. The theoretical framing also has rough edges — Figure 1 labels a contiguous interval of the input domain as the "preimage domain," which conflates a set-theoretic preimage with a continuous block of inputs and reads as imprecise.

On originality, the contribution is real but incremental, and its boundaries against prior work are not drawn cleanly. That high stitching alignment can be misleading is already established in Smith et al.; the IRI/metamer machinery closely relates to Nanda et al. (2022); and the strategy of leveraging robustness to avoid inflated similarity echoes Jones et al. (2022), whose methodology is drawn upon but not fully credited. Engagement with adjacent work on metamers and on the gap between representational and functional similarity is incomplete, and at least one attribution (the representational-versus-functional distinction, arguably due to Csiszárik et al.) appears miscredited. A sharper positioning statement would considerably strengthen the paper. Finally, the empirical scope is somewhat narrow — relatively small models and datasets (CIFAR-10, LS-ImageNet-style) with only a few seeds — so it is unclear how far the conclusions transfer to mainstream modern models; several claims (e.g., in Section 3.4) are also not cross-referenced to the supporting appendix material, and no code or data is released. A substantive open question remains as well: even the invariance-aware objectives (FuLA, SLM) report fairly high similarity when trained on clean data, and it is worth explaining why, and whether the forward-backward requirement is being tested directly or only in aggregate given the IRI training setup.
key_questions_for_authors:
1. What is the central contribution, stated explicitly and early — is it primarily conceptual (the forward-backward compatibility requirement), methodological (the IRI-based training setup and FuLA), or empirical (the comparison across objectives)? And which formulations exactly count as "invariance-aware"?
2. How does the approach meaningfully depart from prior findings that stitching alignment can be high despite different cues (Smith et al.) and from the IRI/metamer machinery of Nanda et al. (2022), as well as the robustness-based similarity methodology of Jones et al. (2022)?
3. Could you clarify the link between the IRI data, forward compatibility, and backward compatibility? In particular, since you appear to replace each sample with a single IRI and train on aggregated behavior rather than on explicit identically-represented pairs, is the forward-backward requirement tested directly or only in aggregate (and how large is the IRI training set relative to the clean one)?
4. Why do FuLA and SLM still yield fairly high similarity when the stitching layer is trained on clean data, and is there a compelling reason to prefer FuLA over the simpler SLM given their similar behavior?
5. Could you formalize the "preimage domain" shading in Figure 1, which currently reads as a conflation of a continuous input interval with a set-theoretic preimage?
limitations: The limitations of scale are only partly acknowledged and could be discussed more directly: the conclusions rest on relatively small-scale image-classification settings with few seeds, so external validity to mainstream models is uncertain. The interpretation also hinges on synthesized IRIs, which are largely unnatural inputs, and the paper lacks a clear oracle notion of when two models should count as functionally similar, making it hard to judge whether the proposed criterion is the "right" one or merely a stricter one. Releasing code and data would further strengthen reproducibility.
